metadata.title:
HOPE union MEMBERS


TELL ABOUT THE FIGHT

pages.raw_text:
ployes to Rathdrum to file a charge
against all the men who had wit
messed the affair.


The company's lawyer trumped up
charge of conspiracy with view
of duplicating all the men In the
union and getting an injunction
against them. Next day the deputy
sheriff arrived from Rathdrum with
warrants for five men.


One of the most important facts
deduced from the testimony Is the
utter absence of reliability In evil
dence given by nonunion men.
The complaining witnesses repeat
edly contradicted themselves, one
drawing a map Of Hope with the
railroad running due north and
south, while every lisping infant IN
the village knows the road runs
southeast and northwest.


The other witness told about
tree six feet from the railroad be-
hind which seven men were hiding
When cross questioned he stated
! there was no tree there.
When the lawyer for the defense
asked him If he were as sure OF
the rest of his testimony as this
he stated he was. To cap the ell
max, he burst into tears and said he
didn't know anything about 1t anc
refused to answer any more ques-
tions from his own lawyer.
Judge McCrea, deeming the evil
dence given as worthless, threw the
| case out of court


Bartlett Sinclair ably defended the
union men,
